# 2 Samuel 24: The King's Numbering and God's Mercy

David's sin in this chapter begins with a census—an act that seems almost innocent on the surface. The Lord stirs him up to number the troops, and David commands Joab to count every fighting man from Dan to Beersheba. Yet even Joab, his ruthless commander, senses something wrong and questions the king's motives. There is no military reason for this count. What David actually wants is to know his own strength, to measure what he has amassed. The act reveals a heart turned inward, assessing power and resources rather than trusting in the God who had delivered him countless times before. Nine months and twenty days of conducting this census across the entire land—a humiliating journey that confirms David's shift from shepherd-king to administrator of his own glory.

When the count returns and David realizes what he has done, the weight of his sin strikes him immediately. This is real repentance, not the half-hearted regret we often mistake for it. David does not rationalize or defend his actions. He does not blame Joab for executing his order or suggest external pressures. He simply says, "I have sinned greatly." Yet the consequences fall not primarily on him but on his people. Seventy thousand die in the plague—soldiers and families, innocent people bearing the price of their king's pride. The angel of destruction advances toward Jerusalem itself, hand raised to strike. Here is the weight of sin made visible: the innocent suffer because of what one person has done. David, watching this devastation, does what a true shepherd does. He throws himself before God, begging that the Lord's hand fall upon him and his house, not on his people.

The path to restoration comes through Gad the prophet and an altar built on Araunah's threshing floor. David refuses to offer what costs him nothing—he insists on paying full price for the land and the sacrifices. This is not the action of a man trying to bargain with God or minimize his obligation. It is the costly response of genuine repentance. He builds an altar and offers burnt offerings and peace offerings, and God answers. The plague stops. What is striking about this moment is that David's restored relationship with the Lord is also the means by which his people are delivered from judgment. This threshing floor will later become the site of the temple, the very center of Israel's worship and the place where God's presence dwells. From the scene of judgment comes the foundation of redemption. The God who disciplines His people in their sin is also the God whose mercies are great, whose relenting hand stays the blow, and whose forgiveness restores both king and kingdom.
